title: L10 Volatility
body:

measurement

 Exponentially Weighted Moving
Average
• In an exponentially weighted moving 2average
  model, the weights assigned to the 𝑢 decline
  exponentially as we move back through time
• This leads to:
                     2              2
             𝜎𝑛2 = 𝜆𝜎𝑛−1 + 1 − 𝜆 𝑢𝑛−1
• Why do we use this?
   •   Little data needs to be stored
   •   Computationally easy
   •   Allows a decay over time
   •   Can be done recursively
• Riskmetrics assumes that 𝜆 = 0.94
 ARCH(m)

• Now suppose we have a view about the ‘long run’ variance.
• We might hypothesize that the variance on day 𝑛 should be a
  combination of that long run variance and the other observed
  deviations over time…
• This gives us:                     𝑚

 GARCH (p,q)
• In GARCH we assign some weight to the long- run average variance rate
• We have 𝑝 numbers of 𝑢 terms and 𝑞 numbers of 𝜎 2 terms

 How do we get these
coefficients?

• The concern is what the coefficient numbers
  should be
• We start with the idea that we want the
  coefficients to be such that we maximize the
  likelihood of our estimate being ‘correct’
• Solver will help us do this
 Example: a binary event
• We are running a casino. We want a rigged game where
  the customer only wins one in 10 times.
• What is our estimate of the proportion of the time, p,
  that it happens?
• The probability of the event happening on one
  particular trial and not on the others is:
                        𝑝𝐴 1 − 𝑝𝐴 9
•   We can then solve for the probability value that
    would maximize this – this cross-validates that
    we want it to payout only 10% of the time
 How do we get a maximum likelihood
estimator here?
• Let’s start with the normal distribution with mean of zero (which is
  what we are using). Denote 𝑣 as the variance. The PDF gives us

 How do we apply this? Solver!
• Start with trial values of ω, α, and β
• Calculate the initial values for 𝜎

• Maximize that value by changing 𝜔, 𝛼, and 𝛽 subject to the
  constraint that 𝜔 + 𝛼 + 𝛽 = 1
 Forecasting volatility

• So far, we have looked one period ahead
• We can look multiple periods ahead
           2
      𝐸 𝜎𝑛+𝑘   = 𝑉𝐿 + 𝛼 + 𝛽 𝑘 𝜎𝑛2 − 𝑉𝐿
• Therefore, the variance for an option expiring in
  𝑚 days is
                     𝑚−1
                  1          2
                     ෍ 𝐸 𝜎𝑛+𝑘
                  𝑚
                     𝑘=0
 What this means for option
pricing
• If we want to obtain an option price, we
  can forecast the relevant volatility using
  the prior formula
• That volatility then becomes the input for
  our BSOPM (or our binomial model as up
  steps are 𝑢 = 𝑒 𝜎 𝑡 and down steps are
  𝑑 = 𝑒 −𝜎 𝑡 )
 This logic also
applies to
correlations
• For example, for an equally
  weighted moving average
  covariance, we have:
  𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑛
  = 𝜆𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑛−1 + 1 − 𝜆 𝑥𝑛−1 𝑦𝑛−1
